summary: The dominant militaristic city-state of ancient Laconia in the southern Peloponnese, ruled by a dual hereditary kingship (the Agiad and Eurypontid dynasties) alongside a council of elders (the gerousia) and five annually elected ephors, all supposedly established by the constitution of the legendary lawgiver Lycurgus. Its citizen population was schooled from age seven in the rigorous agoge training system and supported economically by a subjugated serf class, the helots. Sparta led the Greek resistance at the Battle of Thermopylae (480 BC), defeated Athens in the Peloponnesian War to win Greek hegemony (404-371 BC), and then lost that hegemony to Thebes at the Battle of Leuctra (371 BC), after which it slowly declined through the Hellenistic and Roman periods while remaining a tourist curiosity for its ancient customs.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Sparta is identified in giveaways: a militaristic Doric city-state in Laconia, on the Peloponnese, whose subjugated underclass (the helots) and rigorous military training system (the agoge) made it famous for austerity and terse, "laconic" speech. Its only colony, founded by exiles known as the Partheniai, was Taras (Tarentum) in Magna Graecia.

clues.clue: A militaristic Doric/Dorian city-state in Laconia on the Peloponnese, which subjugated the neighboring region of Messenia and enslaved a servile population called the helots
examples: name this Peloponnesian city that subjugated the helots.
name this Doric city whose constitution didn't protect the rights of captured serfs known as helots.

clues.clue: Famous for its "laconic" terse wit — a mother told a departing soldier to return "either with this or upon this" (his shield); leaders reportedly answered a threat from Philip II with the single word "if"
examples: Plutarch's Moralia features the "sayings" of this city's women, which included the injunction to come back either "with" a shield or "on it."
The leaders of this city supposedly responded with the single word, "if," to a threat from Philip II, an example of its legendary wit.

clues.clue: Hosted the naked/nude gymnopaedia festival, at which women may have competed, and boys were ritually whipped in a Roman-era theater
examples: Women may have competed in this city's naked gymnopaedia festival.
A theater built during Roman rule in this city made public the ritual whipping of boys as part of the Gymnopaedia.

clues.clue: Men and women performed a dance called the bibasis, kicking their own buttocks with their heels as rapidly as possible (a record of 1,000 reps is recorded)
examples: Both men and women from this city would repeatedly kick their own buttocks as quickly as possible in a dance known as the bibasis.
While recalling an activity in this city, Julius Pollux described a woman jumping a record 1,000 times.

clues.clue: Its port was Gythium/Gytheio, through which most of its trade passed; Byzantine writers later connected the nearby town of Mystras to this city
examples: This city's closest port was Gythium, and Byzantine writers frequently connected Mystras to this city.

clues.clue: Legendarily ruled by Menelaus and Helen just before the Trojan War; Helen's suitors swore an oath over a sacrificed horse near this city to protect whichever of them married her
examples: name this city ruled by Menelaus and Helen just before the Trojan War.
Near this city, a horse was sacrificed and atop the pieces of its carcass, a bevy of suitors swore an oath to protect whoever of them was successful.

works.name: Government: Dual Kings, Gerousia, and Ephors
works.description: Sparta's mixed constitution centered on two hereditary kings, drawn simultaneously from the Agiad and Eurypontid dynasties (both claiming descent from Heracles), who shared power with a 28-member council of elders (the gerousia) and five annually elected ephors who held real day-to-day authority, including the power to fine or depose the kings.

clues.clue: Two hereditary kings ruled jointly, one each from the Agiad and Eurypontid dynasties, both claiming descent from Heracles; heirs apparent were uniquely exempt from the agoge
examples: This city was ruled by the Agiad and Eurypontid dynasties, and its constitution was devised by Lykourgos.
Two men from the Agiadai and Eurypontidai families jointly held this position, whose heirs apparent were uniquely exempt from the agoge training program.

clues.clue: The gerousia was a council of 28 elders over age sixty (plus the two kings), elected for life by the loudness of the shouts of their supporters
examples: This city was governed by a council of twenty-eight elders called the gerousia.
Holders of this position served on a judicial council with 28 men over the age of sixty, who were elected based on how loud their supporters yelled.

clues.clue: The five ephors were annually elected officials who made a ceremonial declaration of war on the helots each autumn, swore monthly oaths, could fine even kings, and (though lacking military authority) sent two of their number to accompany every campaign
examples: This city's government included five elders called ephoroi.
Though they lacked military authority, two holders of this position accompanied every campaign.

clues.clue: The constitution, called the Great Rhetra, was supposedly received from the Delphic oracle and established the gerousia and ephorate
examples: This city's Great Rhetra established rule by the gerousia and the ephors and was supposedly given by Lycurgus.
Twenty-eight old men from this city formed the gerousia assembly, one of a number of bodies formed after the Great Rhetra was received from Delphi.

clues.clue: Full citizens who had completed the agoge made up the Apella (Assembly), which elected the ephors and gerousia and approved royal succession
examples: The Spartan ephors presided over this body, which comprised all Spartan men who had passed the agoge. This body was responsible for electing ephors as well as members of the gerousia and kings.

clues.clue: Kings rode to war accompanied by statues of the Dioscuri (Castor and Pollux)
examples: Identify these individuals, who rode to war accompanied by statues of the Dioscuri.

works.name: Lycurgus and the Great Rhetra
works.description: Sparta traced its entire social order back to the possibly legendary lawgiver Lycurgus, credited with instituting the Great Rhetra, the agoge, the gerousia, land redistribution, and even mandatory female athletics — a tradition modern scholars (Hodkinson especially) have significantly complicated.

clues.clue: Legendary lawgiver credited with founding the agoge, the gerousia, and Sparta's austere, militaristic, egalitarian constitution
examples: Sparta traced its heritage back to this legendary lawgiver, who emphasized the virtues of equality, austerity, and military fitness.
identify this position created by Lycurgus, the elected leaders of Sparta.

clues.clue: Aristotle and Herodotus disagreed over whether Lycurgus or Theopompus established the system of ephors
examples: Aristotle and Herodotus disagreed over whether Theompompus or Lykourgos established this city's system of judges known as ephors.

clues.clue: Supposedly allotted each full citizen an equal land grant called a kleros from birth — a tradition Steve Hodkinson's Property and Wealth in Classical Sparta debunks
examples: Each full citizen of this city was given a land grant called a kleros.
Steve Hodkinson's book on Property and Wealth in [This City] debunks the idea that every man in this city was given a plot of land from birth known as a kleros.

clues.clue: Mandated athletic competition for women, according to Xenophon's Constitution of the Lacedaemonians
examples: According to Xenophon's "constitution of the [people of this city]," athletic competitions for women were introduced by the mythical Lycurgus.
Female participation in athletics was mandated by this Spartan ruler so that they would be strong for childbirth.

clues.clue: A later, separate King Lycurgus overthrew the dual monarchy in 215 BC
examples: Name this dynasty which ruled as part of a dual monarchy until 215 BC, when it was overthrown by Lycurgus.

works.name: The Agoge and Spartan Military Society
works.description: All male Spartan citizens (except heirs apparent) were removed from their families around age seven to undergo the agoge, a state-run training regimen lasting into their late twenties that produced Greece's most feared hoplite army and stratified citizens into a strict hierarchy of full "peers," disgraced "tremblers," and other subordinate classes.

clues.clue: State-run training and education system for boys, beginning around age seven, that produced Sparta's citizen army and culminated around age twenty
examples: Name this city-state whose male citizens lived in barracks from the ages of 7 to 29 as part of its agoge training system.
All male citizens of Sparta underwent this education and training program.

clues.clue: Participants deliberately stole cheese from the altar of Artemis Orthia and were ritually whipped/flogged if caught, joining syssition dining groups upon completion
examples: Participants in this system deliberately stole cheese from the altar of Orthia and joined syssition dining groups after its completion.
In a ritual conducted at the Sanctuary of Artemis Orthia, Spartan youth were flogged while they attempted to steal this foodstuff from the altar.

clues.clue: Trainees were given a single red cloak per year and pulled reeds by hand from the Eurotas River to sleep on
examples: People undergoing this process were given a single red cloak each year, and pulled reeds out by hand from the Eurotas river.

clues.clue: Culminated in the krypteia, a secret-police rite in which graduates could kill helots with impunity after an annual declaration of war by the ephors
examples: In a violent part of this process called the krypteia, participants in this process had to kill a helot with impunity.
After completing the Agoge, young Spartans could participate in this rite, which involved wandering around the countryside and knifing helots.

clues.clue: Adult male citizens dined together in mandatory communal messes called syssitia, where excessive drinking was prohibited (unlike the looser symposia elsewhere in Greece)
examples: Name this practice in which men would hold a daily banquet with their fellow soldiers. Prohibition of excessive drinking at these events meant they were usually tamer than the similar symposia.
Membership in a syssitia was granted after completing this process.

clues.clue: Full citizens who completed the agoge were called homoioi ("peers"); those who failed to pay syssitia dues lost rights and became hypomeiones
examples: This city was home to a class of people who did not benefit from inheriting property called the hypomeiones.
Those full citizens of this city, called "peers" or homoioi, were required to participate in a secret police called krypteia during their youth.

clues.clue: Cowards (tresantes) were forced to wear a ratty, multicolored patched cloak and shave off half their beard; non-citizen boys who trained alongside citizens became mothakes/mothones
examples: A ratty tunic patched with cloth of many colors was the required garb of another social group in this city, who were forced to shave half their beards and called tresantes.
Mothones were members of these people who had completed the agoge.

clues.clue: The skiritai, a light-infantry unit of roughly 600 men, took their name from the mountainous region near Sparta where they lived
examples: A rustic group from near this city took their name from the characteristic leather tunics they wore as a light infantry cohort which typically numbered 600 men.
This city named its peasant skiritai class after the mountainous region in which many of them lived.

clues.clue: The gerousia decided whether infants born unfit would be raised or exposed to die
examples: Name this education system under which youth received 22 years of training. The Gerousia would determine if infants could be brought up in this system or if they should be exposed to die.

works.name: Women of Sparta
works.description: Unusually among Greek city-states, Spartan women trained athletically, could own and inherit property, and are the subject of a substantial ancient and modern literature (chiefly Sarah Pomeroy's scholarship) — a status Aristotle blamed for the city's eventual decline.

clues.clue: Unlike other Greek women, Spartan women were allowed to own and inherit property; Aristotle's Politics blames this "license" for the city's decline
examples: Aristotle attributes this city's decline to the fact that it allowed women to own property.
In Book II of Politics, Aristotle critiques the "license" of these people, whose powers he says "give an air of indecorum" to constitutional order.

clues.clue: Women wore short, knee-length chitons (earning the epithet "thigh-showers") and exercised/competed athletically, including in the gymnopaedia
examples: Identify these people who, unusually for their time, were allowed to own land. They wore knee-length chitons rather than ankle-length ones during races.

clues.clue: Unmarried women sang the partheneia ("maiden-songs") of the poet Alcman, including the "First Partheneion"
examples: Many votive lead figurines were dedicated at the sanctuary of Artemis Orthia... the poems of Tyrtaeus encouraged this city in its wars.
The "First Partheneion" was meant to be sung by these people and was penned by Alcman.

clues.clue: Sarah Pomeroy wrote the first book-length history of Spartan women, noting they continued wearing the peplos for centuries after it was abandoned elsewhere in Greece
examples: Sarah Pomeroy wrote the first book-length history of women in this city.
In a 2002 monograph on women in this city, Sarah Pomeroy discusses how its women wore the peplos for hundreds of years after it was abandoned elsewhere.

clues.clue: Queen Gorgo, daughter of Agiad king Cleomenes I, was among the women whose sharp "sayings" Plutarch recorded in the Moralia
examples: This city's queen Gorgo was the daughter of the Agiad king Cleomenes I.
The 18th book of Plutarch's Moralia records their "Sayings."

clues.clue: Cynisca was the first woman to win an event at the Olympic Games; Archidamia resisted evacuation before Pyrrhus's invasion by appearing before the assembly with a sword in hand
examples: A popular cult figure among these people, Cynisca, was the first of them to win an event at the Olympic Games.
A leader of these people resisted a call for their evacuation to Crete by appearing before an assembly "with sword in hand."

clues.clue: Plutarch claims newborns were bathed in wine, believed to euthanize epileptic or sickly infants
examples: Plutarch wrote that women from this city used wine to bathe newborns in order to euthanize epileptic children.

works.name: Helots, Perioikoi, and the Messenian Wars
works.description: Sparta's economy rested on the forced labor of the helots, a state-owned serf population (mainly Messenian, reportedly some 90% of the total population) created by the conquest of Messenia in a series of Messenian Wars, while a separate class of free but non-citizen perioikoi handled trade and crafts.

clues.clue: State-owned serf class, descended largely from the conquered population of Messenia (with some from Laconia), that farmed for Sparta's citizens and made up as much as 90% of the population
examples: name this servile class of Sparta.
This was the term for those slaves, who made up 90% of the Spartan population.

clues.clue: Every autumn the ephors ritually declared war on the helots, licensing the krypteia to kill them with impunity (Thucydides: Spartan policy was "always determined by the necessity of taking precautions against" them)
examples: Every autumn, the ephors declared war on these people.
According to Thucydides, one city's policy was "always determined by the necessity of taking precautions against" these people.

clues.clue: Could earn freedom as neodamodeis by serving as hoplites (or by paying 500 drachmas); Brasidas recruited a unit of these freed helots for his Chalcidice campaign, nicknamed "Brasidians"
examples: These people could become neodamodes after serving as hoplites or undergoing the agoge educational system.
700 members of this group who served in Chalcidice became known as "Brasidians" for the name of their commander, Brasidas.

clues.clue: The First Messenian War (c. 735-715 BC) subjugated Messenia; sons born to unmarried women during the war (the Partheniai) were denied property rights and left to found Taras (Tarentum) in Magna Graecia — Sparta's only colony
examples: The children of unmarried women from this city, called "sons of virgins" or Partheniai, founded the city of Taras in Magna Graecia, which was this city's only colony.
Several historians agree that the Partheniae were expelled from Sparta during the first of these bloody wars, which lasted from about 735 to 715 BC.

clues.clue: The poet Tyrtaeus wrote poems urging on Sparta's Messenian wars
examples: The poems of Tyrtaeus encouraged this city in its wars with Messenia.

clues.clue: The Third Messenian War was sparked by a devastating earthquake in 464 BC; helot survivors fortified themselves on Mount Ithome and, after a failed relief expedition by Athens's Cimon (which led to his 461 BC ostracism), were resettled by Athens at Naupactus
examples: An earthquake that struck this city in 464 BCE coincided with a revolt whose survivors settled at Naupactos.
Cimon was sent to help by Athens following an earthquake and subsequent revolt of its helots, who fortified themselves on Mt Ithome in Messenia.

clues.clue: According to Thucydides, about 2,000 helots selected as the bravest were secretly executed after being told they would be freed
examples: According to Thucydides, 2,000 of these people were executed in the wake of an earthquake at Cape Taenarus.
Part of this group was given garlands and slain at the temple of Poseidon at Taenarus.

clues.clue: Free but non-citizen residents of Laconia, the perioikoi controlled trade and business and fought as hoplites alongside the Spartiates, but held no political rights
examples: Though they were free and controlled business in Sparta, unlike the helots, these residents of Laconia were not considered citizens.
Only full Spartan citizens could take part in the Assembly, a designation that excluded both free perioikoi and this class of state-owned laborers.

clues.clue: The populist Conspiracy of Cinadon, provoked by rising inequality worsened by the Law of Epitadeus, was put down by the government
examples: This city's government put down the populist Conspiracy of Cinadon, provoked by rising inequality here that worsened with the Law of Epitadeus.

works.name: Thermopylae, the Persian Wars, and Rivalry with Argos
works.description: In the Archaic and early Classical periods Sparta repeatedly clashed with neighboring Argos over the border region of Thyreatis/Cynuria, before leading the mainland Greek resistance to Persia at the Battle of Thermopylae under King Leonidas.

clues.clue: King Leonidas led roughly 300 Spartans (plus allies) to a last stand against Xerxes's Persian invasion at Thermopylae (480 BC); Simonides's epitaph reads "go tell the Spartans... we lie here, obedient to their words"
examples: name this city, which retained cultural influence through the legacy of leaders like Leonidas.
Name this home city of Tyrtaeus. An epigram by Simonides asks, "Stranger, go tell" this city's inhabitants that "we lie here, obedient to their words."

clues.clue: Agiad king Cleomenes I crushed Argos at the Battle of Sepeia (c. 494 BC), burning down a sacred grove where Argive survivors had taken refuge and killing much of Argos's male citizenry
examples: One of these powers crushed the other at Sepeia.
Another mass slaughter of male citizens of that one of these two cities occurred at Sepiae, when one of the kings of the other city, Cleomenes I, burned down a sacred grove.

clues.clue: The Battle of the Champions (c. 546 BC) pitted 300 chosen Spartans against 300 chosen Argives over the disputed territory of Thyreatis ("an area no bigger than an Egyptian bean"); the ambiguous result led to a full battle
examples: A source claims these powers fought over an area "no bigger than an Egyptian bean" known as Cynuria.
they may be better known for a fight in which they each chose 300 soldiers to battle each other, ending with one man claiming victory because he despoiled the dead.

clues.clue: An earlier, disputed Battle of Hysiae (known only from Pausanias) may have been linked to hoplite reforms by a tyrant of one of the two cities; the entire male population of Hysiae was said to have been slaughtered
examples: Because a battle was mentioned only by the travel writer Pausanias, historians question whether these powers fought at the Battle of Hysiae while one of them was ruled by Pheidon.
That battle saw the entire male population of Hysiae slaughtered.

clues.clue: Sparta and Argos vied for control of the Peloponnese prior to 500 BC
examples: name these two city-states that, prior to 500 BCE, vied for control of the Peloponnesus.

works.name: The Peloponnesian War and Spartan Hegemony
works.description: Sparta led the Peloponnesian League against the Athenian-led Delian League in the Peloponnesian War, winning at Aegospotami and installing the Thirty Tyrants in Athens, then held Greek hegemony from 404 to 371 BC through military governors and client oligarchies until its overreach provoked renewed conflict.

clues.clue: Led the Peloponnesian League against Athens's Delian League in the Peloponnesian War; won Greek hegemony (404-371 BC) after admiral Lysander destroyed the Athenian fleet at Aegospotami (405 BC)
examples: name this Greek city-state whose hegemony lasted from 404 to 371 B.C., after its victory in the Peloponnesian War.
Lysander, one of its admirals, smashed the Athenian fleet at Aegospotami to end the Peloponnesian War.

clues.clue: After defeating Athens, razed the Long Walls and installed the oligarchic Thirty Tyrants there
examples: That battle at Leuctra ended the hegemony of this city, which razed the Long Walls and installed the Thirty Tyrants after defeating Athens in the Peloponnesian War.
This city later installed the Thirty Tyrants after winning the Peloponnesian War.

clues.clue: Controlled its new empire through military governors called harmosts and imposed oligarchic "decarchies" (councils of ten) on the Aegean islands
examples: This city dispatched military governors called harmosts to newly conquered territories.
This city subordinated the Aegean Islands by appointing harmost-led "decarchies" to control their cities.

clues.clue: Spartan hoplites were besieged and surrendered on the island of Sphacteria near Pylos (425 BC); their captured shields were displayed as war trophies in Athens's Stoa Poikile
examples: The Stoa Poikile once held the shields of soldiers from this city who were stranded on an island after the Battle of Pylos.

clues.clue: The Corinthian War ended with the King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas), negotiated by a Spartan diplomat, which handed Persian king Artaxerxes II control over the Greek cities of Asia Minor
examples: A diplomat from this city negotiated a treaty within which King Artaxerxes II gained control over the Greeks of Asia; that Peace of Antalcidas ended the Corinthian War.

clues.clue: Overreach during hegemony: the acquittal of Sphodrias for seizing a foreign port was dubbed the city's "most unjust verdict," and its forces were ambushed at Lechaeum by Athenian peltasts under Iphicrates
examples: The acquittal of Sphodrias after he tried to seize a port was dubbed this city's "most unjust verdict." Forces from this city were ambushed at Lechaeum by peltasts that were led by Iphicrates.

clues.clue: Allied with Amyntas III of Macedon to destroy the Chalcidian League, provoking anger over Phoebidas's and Sphodrias's illegal seizures of foreign territory
examples: This city tried to secure itself against enemies by allying with Amyntas III to tear down the Chalcidian League, and by supporting Phoebidas and Sphodrias' illegal land seizures from foreign powers.

works.name: Battle of Leuctra and the Rise of Thebes
works.description: Sparta's hegemony collapsed in 371 BC when the Theban general Epaminondas, using an unprecedented "oblique order" phalanx formation, crushed the Spartan army at Leuctra and killed King Cleombrotus I, ending nearly four decades of Spartan dominance and opening the way for Theban ascendancy.

clues.clue: Thebes, led by Epaminondas (with Pelopidas and the Sacred Band), destroyed Sparta's army at the Battle of Leuctra (371 BC) using the "oblique order" — an extra-deep left flank (fifty ranks)
examples: This city was defeated by Pelopidas and Epaminondas at the Battle of Leuctra, a victory for Thebes.
This man introduced the oblique order of battle by stacking his left flank fifty ranks deep in a battle against Cleombrotus.

clues.clue: King Cleombrotus I was killed in Epaminondas's attack on the Spartan right wing at Leuctra
examples: The first Spartan king of this name was killed during Epaminondas's unconventional attack on the Spartan right wing in the Battle of Leuctra.

clues.clue: Leuctra ended Sparta's roughly 33-year hegemony and permanently broke its military reputation; Sparta's earlier defeat at the smaller Battle of Tegyra had already taught Thebes the tactics used there
examples: This city's excessive foreign adventuring taught its enemies how to defeat it at the Battle of Tegyra, a precursor to a battle in which another city used a phalanx with an extra-deep left wing to destroy this city's army.

clues.clue: Agesilaus II, a prominent Spartan king during the hegemony period, was opposed by an alliance at the Second Battle of Mantinea (362 BC), the last battle between Sparta and Thebes, where Epaminondas was killed
examples: An alliance at the Second Battle of Mantinea opposed this city's ruler Agesilaus II.
That leader, Epaminondas, was killed at the last battle between them.

clues.clue: After Leuctra, Epaminondas invaded the Peloponnese, dissolved part of Sparta's helot system, and the Arcadians founded the fortified city of Megalopolis to defend against Sparta
examples: Epaminondas dissolved one group of these people after the Battle of Leuctra.
A leader of one of these cities ordered the creation of the Megalopolis to defend against the other.

works.name: Hellenistic and Roman Sparta
works.description: A militarily diminished but culturally distinctive Sparta survived into the Hellenistic and Roman eras, resisting Pyrrhus of Epirus, briefly resurging under the reformer-tyrant Nabis before submission to the Achaean League and Rome, and later becoming a curiosity destination for Roman tourists drawn to its ancient customs.

clues.clue: In 272 BC, Pyrrhus of Epirus besieged Sparta in a failed attempt to install the exiled Cleonymus as king, exploiting the agoge's practice of training foreign trophimoi; a Spartan woman leader (Archidamia) resisted evacuation, appearing before the assembly "with sword in hand"
examples: Pyrrhus of Epirus disastrously attempted to install Cleonymus as king of this city.
Pyrrhus of Epirus allegedly launched a 272 BCE siege to take advantage of this system for foreign trophimoi.

clues.clue: The last independent king, Nabis, freed and armed some 12,000 slaves to build an army; he was defeated in the War Against Nabis by the Achaean League (backed by Rome), which forced Sparta's submission
examples: The War Against Nabis pitted the Achaean League against this city-state, which trained soldiers in the brutal agoge system.
This city was subdued with the defeat of Nabis.

clues.clue: Nabis reportedly got debtors drunk, then killed them with "Apega," an iron-maiden-like device disguised as his own beautiful wife
examples: A ruler of this city is said to have enjoyed getting debtors drunk and then setting up an iron maiden-like device disguised as his promiscuous wife.
Sarah Pomeroy... describes an attempt to build a "female robot as evil and deceptive as Pandora" dressed in the jewels of the evil wife Apega.

clues.clue: Under Rome, Augustus visited the city to reward Eurycles for his support at Actium and ate with common soldiers to honor its traditional customs; foreigners could be granted the honorary office of patronomos
examples: When Augustus visited this city to reward Eurycles for supporting him, he ate with its common soldiers to show approval of its traditional ways.
To encourage patronage for this city, foreigners were occasionally granted the honorary position of patronomos.

clues.clue: During the Hellenistic and Roman periods, foreigners visited chiefly for its ancient customs supposedly derived from the Laws of Lycurgus
examples: Foreigners' interest in visiting this city during the Hellenistic and Roman periods came primarily from its customs derived from the Laws of Lycurgus.

clues.clue: 1 Maccabees describes (likely fabricated) correspondence between Sparta and the Hasmoneans, per historian Erich Gruen
examples: 1 Maccabees describes correspondence between this city and the Hasmoneans, which Erich Gruen argued was fabricated to promote Jewish culture.

works.name: Legacy: The "Spartan Mirage" and Historiography
works.description: Because so much of what survives about Sparta comes from admiring outsiders, modern historians speak of a "Spartan mirage" — an idealized image that scholarship from François Ollier through Paul Cartledge, Elizabeth Rawson, Steve Hodkinson, and Paul Rahe has worked to separate from reality.

clues.clue: The idealized, largely mythologized image of Sparta constructed by ancient admirers and later Europeans is termed the "Spartan mirage" — the subject of a book by French historian François Ollier
examples: This city's idealization by historians was evaluated in a Francois Ollier book about [this city's] "Mirage."

clues.clue: Elizabeth Rawson's 1969 book traced the influence of Sparta's "Tradition" on 19th- and 20th-century European thought
examples: A 1969 book by Elizabeth Rawson traces the influence of this city's "Tradition" on European thought in the 19th and 20th centuries.

clues.clue: Historian Paul Cartledge was made an honorary citizen of Sparta in 2005, having written a 1989 book examining it in its "Hellenistic and Roman" eras
examples: In 2005, historian Paul Cartledge was made an honorary citizen of this city for books such as a 1989 text examining it in "Hellenistic and Roman" eras.

clues.clue: Historian Paul A. Rahe is writing a six-volume (hexalogy) history of Sparta
examples: Rahe is writing a hexalogy on the history of this city.

clues.clue: Renaissance interest was rare — Cyriac of Ancona was one of the only Renaissance scholars to explore Sparta, though his writings weren't published until centuries after his death
examples: One of the only Renaissance scholars to explore this city was Cyriac of Ancona, although his extensive writings about it weren't published until centuries after his death.

clues.clue: Historian Irad Malkin argued that a Spartan founder of a short-lived colony at Kinyps had territorial ambitions reaching west to Lake Tritonis; a cult of Apollo has been seen as "forging a chain" linking Sparta to its colonies of Thera and Cyrene
examples: Irad Malkin argued that a man from this city who founded a short-lived colony at Kinyps must have had territorial ambitions extending all the way west to Lake Tritonis.

comprehensive_summary: Sparta was the dominant military power of the southern Peloponnese, an austere Doric city-state in Laconia governed by an unusual mixed constitution supposedly instituted by the legendary lawgiver Lycurgus. Two hereditary kings, drawn simultaneously from the Agiad and Eurypontid dynasties (both claiming descent from Heracles), shared power with the gerousia — a council of 28 elders over sixty, elected for life by acclamation — and with five annually elected ephors, who wielded the real day-to-day power, including the authority to fine even the kings; the whole system rested on the Great Rhetra, a constitution reputedly handed down by the Delphic oracle. Every Spartan male citizen except an heir apparent was removed from his family around age seven to undergo the agoge, a brutal, decades-long training regimen involving ritualized cheese-theft from the altar of Artemis Orthia, communal dining in syssitia, and a stratified hierarchy separating full citizens (homoioi) from disgraced "tremblers" (tresantes), impoverished hypomeiones, and light-infantry skiritai; graduates could join the krypteia, a secret-police rite empowered to kill helots with impunity after the ephors' annual ritual declaration of war on them. Unusually for Greek women, Spartan women trained athletically, wore short chitons, sang Alcman's partheneia, and could own and inherit property — a fact Aristotle blamed for the city's decline, and the subject of extensive modern scholarship by Sarah Pomeroy; notable women included Queen Gorgo, daughter of Cleomenes I, the Olympic victor Cynisca, and Archidamia, who resisted evacuation before Pyrrhus's siege. Sparta's economy depended on the helots, a state-owned serf population — reportedly as much as 90% of the total population — created chiefly by the conquest of Messenia in the Messenian Wars: the First Messenian War produced the Partheniai, sons of unmarried women denied inheritance who left to found Sparta's only colony, Taras (Tarentum); the Third Messenian War was touched off by a devastating 464 BC earthquake and helot revolt centered on Mount Ithome, whose survivors Athens (after Cimon's failed relief expedition, which triggered his ostracism) resettled at Naupactus. A separate free but non-citizen class, the perioikoi, handled trade. Militarily, Sparta repeatedly clashed with Argos over the border territory of Thyreatis (the Battle of the Champions, the Battle of Sepeia, and the disputed Battle of Hysiae) before leading the mainland Greek resistance to Persia at Thermopylae (480 BC), where King Leonidas and roughly 300 Spartans died to the last man. Sparta then led the Peloponnesian League to victory over Athens's Delian League in the Peloponnesian War, winning at Aegospotami under Lysander, installing the oligarchic Thirty Tyrants in Athens, and holding Greek hegemony from 404 to 371 BC through military governors (harmosts) and imposed "decarchies" — hegemony marred by episodes like the Sphodrias affair and the Corinthian War, ended by the Peace of Antalcidas. That hegemony collapsed in 371 BC at the Battle of Leuctra, where the Theban general Epaminondas's innovative "oblique order" phalanx destroyed the Spartan army and killed King Cleombrotus I; a second Theban victory at Mantinea (362 BC, where Epaminondas himself died) confirmed Sparta's decline. In the Hellenistic era Sparta repelled Pyrrhus of Epirus's 272 BC siege before the reformer-king Nabis, who armed thousands of freed slaves, was crushed by the Achaean League in the War Against Nabis, ending Spartan independence; under Rome, emperors like Augustus rewarded local elites such as Eurycles and Sparta became a tourist curiosity for customs "derived from the Laws of Lycurgus." Because so much surviving evidence comes from admiring outsiders, modern historians (Ollier, Rawson, Cartledge, Hodkinson, Rahe, Pomeroy) have worked to separate the idealized "Spartan mirage" from historical reality.
recursive_suggestions: Battle of Thermopylae / Leonidas — has enough independent clue material for its own topic; note both events (Sepeia at diff. periods) can be conflated in trivia-chain bonus clues about "this king"
Battle of Leuctra / Epaminondas — Theban general with independent tossup presence; watch for overlap between Sparta's and a future Thebes page's Leuctra sections
Peloponnesian War — has substantial independent presence as its own answerline; category filter to avoid double-counting clues here vs. a dedicated page
Agesilaus II / Lysander / Cleomenes I / Nabis — recurring Spartan kings/generals with enough distinct material to become their own topics if they recur further
Helots / Agoge / Ephors / Gerousia — each already has independent answerline presence (captured above) and could be split into standalone topics under a broader "Ancient Institutions" queue
Thebes — recurring rival city (Leuctra, Epaminondas, Pelopidas, Boeotian League); excluded here except where directly clashing with Sparta
Argos — recurring rival city (Sepeia, Battle of the Champions, Hysiae); excluded here except where directly clashing with Sparta

Achaean League — DIFFERENT topic; only its War Against Nabis conflict with Sparta was included here, per-answerline clues about the League itself (Critolaus, Lucius Mummius, dissolution in 146 BC) were excluded
Sardis — a false-positive fetch result (capital of Lydia under Croesus); entirely unrelated to Sparta, excluded

Spartacus / Spartacus League / Spartacist Uprising / "I am Spartacus!" — false-positive fetch results matched on the substring "Sparta"; the Roman gladiator Spartacus and the German Marxist Spartacist movement are wholly unrelated topics and were excluded entirely
Helen of Troy / "ending the Peloponnesian War" (Lysistrata) — Literature/Mythology answerlines that happen to be set in or reference Sparta; excluded per the no-outside-category rule
aspartate / aspartic acid / caspases — biochemistry answerlines that matched only via the "Sparta"/"aspart-" substring; wholly unrelated and excluded
